reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents Augmented Policy Cloning (APC), a simple yet effective data augmentation strategy for policy cloning in reinforcement and imitation learning. In APC, Gaussian noise is added to states drawn from expert rollouts, and the expert policy is queried at these perturbed states to label them with new actions. A student policy is then trained on the union of original and synthetic (state, action) pairs. The method is evaluated across three high-degree-of-freedom continuous control tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite (Humanoid Run, Humanoid Walls, and Insert Peg) and in several cloning settings: standard behavior cloning (BC), expert compression, privileged-expert transfer, DAgger, and kickstarting. APC consistently outperforms baselines—standard BC and a naive augmented BC (Naive ABC) that perturbs states but reuses original actions—achieving similar or better performance with significantly fewer expert trajectories or environment interactions.

Strengths:
1. Clarity and Depth of Experiments: The paper is very well written and thoroughly documented. Sections 4 and 5 provide extensive implementation details—policy architectures, noise levels (σE=0.2, σ=0.2), batch sizes, and Algorithm 1’s pseudocode—enabling reproducibility. The ablation in Appendix 6 on short versus full-length trajectories, and the detailed study in Section 5.2 on varying student network sizes ([256], [256,64], [256,256], [256,256,256]), effectively demonstrate APC’s robustness to architectural constraints.
2. Empirical Effectiveness: Across all tasks in Figure 2, APC requires fewer expert trajectories to match teacher performance; Naive ABC seldom improves on BC, confirming that querying the expert is essential. In expert compression (Figure 3), APC maintains high episodic rewards even when the student torso has 1/256 the parameters of the expert. In privileged-expert transfer (Figure 4), APC bridges the gap between state-only and vision-only students, while BC and Naive ABC fail to learn.
3. Broad Applicability: Beyond offline BC, APC integrates naturally into DAgger (Section 6.1, Figure 5) and kickstarting (Section 6.2, Figure 6), showing gains even in online, mixture-policy regimes with mixing coefficients β=0 and β=0.3. The fact that APC can be plugged into multiple expert-driven frameworks highlights its versatility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Limited Novelty Relative to Prior Label-Augmentation Work:
   – The core idea—augmenting states and querying an expert to relabel them—is straightforward, and prior works in the imitation-learning and autonomous driving literature have applied similar observation perturbations with expert correction [Bojarski et al., 2016; George et al., 2018; Buhet et al., 2019]. The manuscript acknowledges these in Section 1 but could strengthen its discussion by contrasting APC’s general-purpose, domain-agnostic noise model with, say, the shift-based image labels in Bojarski, which rely on known camera transformations.
   – I recommend the authors more explicitly position APC relative to those label-correction methods, perhaps in a dedicated “Related Work” subsection, and highlight how APC’s reliance solely on expert queries (without requiring a model of perturbations) generalizes across continuous state and pixel-based observations.
2. Motivation and Real-World Examples:
   – While the paper states that querying an expert policy is “cheap” compared to collecting new trajectories and mentions “memory constraints” for large transformers in distillation [Parisotto and Salakhutdinov, 2021], no concrete scenario is provided. It would strengthen the motivation to cite a real robotics or autonomous-driving case where simulator or oracle rollout is slow or unsafe, yet expert inference is fast—e.g., distilling a GPU-resident model to an edge device for closed-loop control.
3. Fairness of Baseline Comparisons:
   – The comparison to BC and Naive ABC is informative, but arguably not sufficient. As the authors note, one could synthetically sample states (even off-trajectory) from a broad distribution—such as Gaussian with mean μ and covariance Σ estimated from expert data—and query the expert on all of them, effectively covering the state space. This baseline would test whether APC’s local, trajectory-centric perturbations are indeed more data-efficient than random coverage.
   – I suggest adding an experiment in which zero expert trajectories are used: all training data are synthetic states drawn from N(μ,Σ) (estimated from expert rollouts) with expert queries. How does APC perform in that setting compared to using no real rollouts?
4. Visual-Domain Perturbations:
   – The paper briefly mentions image cropping and color-augmentation for the Walls task (Sec. 4.2) in vision-only variants. However, it remains unclear why Gaussian perturbations on state-derived inputs produce useful invariances in pixel space. An intuition or small experiment—e.g., applying synthetic camera jitter on images and re-querying a vision-based expert—would clarify how APC extends to purely vision-based imitation.
5. Presentation Minor Fixes:
   – Figure 1 and Algorithm 1 are first displayed several pages before the text refers to them; relocating or cross-referencing them earlier (e.g., at the start of Section 3) would improve the flow.
   – In Section 6.1, when using DAgger with Gaussian experts and students, it is noted that replacing log π in eqn. (4) with state-conditional cross-entropy H[πE‖π] yielded better performance (Appendix 8.6). A brief mention in the main text would help readers replicate results.

Questions:
1. In purely visual imitation tasks, many perturbed images may lie off-manifold. Do the authors have intuition or empirical evidence on the regions of pixel-space where APC remains effective?
2. How does APC perform with zero real trajectories—i.e., when all training samples are synthetic states drawn from N(μ,Σ) estimated from expert rollouts—and only expert queries?
3. Could the authors provide specific real-world examples or cite robotics use cases where querying an expert policy is inexpensive relative to trajectory collection?

Limitations:
The authors rightly point out in Section 7 that APC requires an expert policy that can be freely queried. In settings where each expert inference is expensive (e.g., human teleoperation or computationally heavy simulations), APC’s advantage might diminish. Providing a concrete cost comparison—such as expert query time versus simulation rollout time—would help practitioners decide when APC is appropriate.

Decision:
6: Weak Accept. The work is technically solid, empirically thorough, and of practical interest. While the core idea is simple and related to prior label-correction methods, the breadth of applications and strong empirical gains across multiple cloning scenarios support acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning algorithms. It consists of adding Gaussian noise to the states used for training and storing the perturbed states and the action the expert policy would produce in such states. Using this augmented dataset, the proposed algorithm achieves better performance than the baselines over three complex continuous tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well written and contains many implementation details and ablation studies.

The idea proposed is very simple and effective. Unfortunately, apart from the experimental results in complex environments, the novelty in this work is limited. It is well known that augmenting the dataset in Behavioral Cloning is useful and there are prior works applying observation perturbation and label corrections via experts queries [1,2,3].

The main motivation for using the proposed approach is the case when collecting more data in the environment is expensive, but querying the expert many times is not. The authors motivate this by saying that "an expert policy may be too large to execute due to memory considerations" and that "Many expert-driven learning approaches actually have access to an expert that can be queried", but no specific example is provided to the reader. 

The comparison with baselines BC and ABC does not seem very fair. If the policy has enough capacity and the distribution from which we sample the states covers the state-space enough, one could clone the expert by repeatedly querying it on such random states without even seeing a single trajectory. Therefore it is quite straightforward that the proposed method works better than the baselines.  

[1] M. Bojarski et al., “End to end learning for self-driving cars,” arXiv preprint, 2016

[2] L. George, T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, G. Le-Gall, and X. Perrotton, “Imitation learning for end to end vehicle longitudinal control with forward camera,” in NeurIPS, 2018.

[3] T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, and X. Perrotton, “Conditional Vehicle Trajectories Prediction in CARLA Urban Environment,” in ICCV, 2019

Questions:
While in continuous control environments adding Gaussian noise to a state often produces a perturbed state that could be reached by an agent (i.e. valid states), it is not the case when operating with visual observations. Is there an intuition why data augmentation would be useful in the visual domain?

Assume that, when the expert was trained, it observes in the environment states with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ (this could be estimated from the last few trajectories during the training of the expert). What is the performance of APC when using zero trajectories and only synthetic data coming from a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$? 


Figure 1 and Algorithm 1 appear many pages before the first time they are mentioned in the paper. I suggest putting them when they are mentioned.

Limitations:
The authors discuss the possible limitations of this work. The method queries the expert many times, so if expert queries are expensive, their approach might suffer. I suggest providing specific examples for this setting.




---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Edit: updating my score after author-reviewer discussion

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a simple yet effective way APC, which queries the parametric experts to augment the demonstrations for imitation learning. APC is shown to outperform naive behavior cloning in both expert compression and transferring from privileged expert settings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Pros:
This paper focuses on a novel problem where a parametric expert can be queried. The paper writing is clear and easy to follow. The empirical results do demonstrate the advantage of APC over BC and naive ABC. 

Cons:
However, I don't feel this paper proposes any novel idea. One serious issue is that when compared with baselines, all these baselines don't query the expert as APC does. This means that the baselines have fewer demonstrations to imitate from. It is clear that APC will beat naive ABC since APC has more high-quality/ground-truth demonstrations. Therefore, I think this doesn't make enough technical contributions to the problem it studies.
reviews.questions:
I think the key problem here is that the authors don't explore the methods of querying experts enough. From my point of view, the advantage of APC is totally brought by a new setting where the expert could be queried, which is not an innovation of the methods. I suggest the authors compare APC with more baselines that can query the experts. 

Although this method is reasonable, I'm afraid the current technical contribution is far from enough. Adding the Gaussian noise is the simplest way to make a data augmentation, and previous works (e.g., RAD, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) also have systematically studied the data augmentations for general reinforcement learning problems. It could be better if the authors come up with novel methods for data augmentations in your settings.
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations.

reviews.text:
Review for “Data augmentation for efficient learning from parametric experts”

Summary:
This paper introduces Augmented Policy Cloning (APC), a straightforward data-augmentation approach that exploits the ability to query a parametric expert policy when performing imitation learning. By perturbing states along expert trajectories with Gaussian noise and then querying the expert at these perturbed states, APC generates “virtual” expert demonstrations without additional rollouts in the environment. The authors evaluate APC across a diverse suite of high-dimensional continuous-control problems drawn from the DeepMind Control Suite—namely Humanoid Run, Humanoid Walls, and Insert Peg—and in multiple learning regimes: offline behavioral cloning, expert compression (student networks with fewer parameters), privileged-expert transfer (expert sees full state, student sees vision), as well as in online subroutines like DAgger and kickstarting. The empirical results (Figures 2–6) consistently show that APC matches or outperforms both standard behavioral cloning (BC) and a naive augmentation baseline (Naive ABC) that perturbs states but reuses original expert actions. APC achieves high performance with far fewer expert trajectories, and proves especially beneficial when compressing large teacher networks or distilling from privileged experts.

Strengths:
• Focus on a realistic setting: The paper addresses the under-explored “policy cloning” scenario where an expert policy is available for online queries, a setting that naturally arises in actor-learner distillation [Parisotto & Salakhutdinov, 2021], privileged-information transfer, DAgger [Ross et al., 2011], and kickstarting [Schmitt et al., 2018].  
• Clarity and presentation: The manuscript is well written, with a clear problem statement (Section 2), a concise algorithmic summary (Algorithm 1), and detailed experimental protocol (Section 4 and Appendices).  
• Broad evaluation: The authors validate APC in both state-only and vision + state domains (Figure 4), across multiple tasks, and demonstrate its benefits in both low-data (offline BC) and online (DAgger, kickstarting) regimes (Figures 5–6). Notably, APC yields near-expert performance with as few as 10–20 expert trajectories on Humanoid Run (Figure 2, left).  
• Practical relevance: In expert-compression settings (Figure 3), where student networks have up to 256× fewer parameters, APC suffers much less performance drop than BC or Naive ABC, highlighting its utility when deployment constraints demand smaller models.

Weaknesses:
• Limited methodological novelty: The key idea—adding Gaussian perturbations to states and querying the expert—is conceptually straightforward and resembles classical noise-injection approaches in control [Laskey et al., 2017; Mordatch & Todorov, 2014]. While the paper positions APC as a generalization of prior label-augmentation methods (Section 1), the core augmentation strategy remains a simple state-space Gaussian shift.  
• Baseline disparity in expert queries: In the experiments, BC and Naive ABC never query the expert at perturbed states, whereas APC does. Consequently, APC inherently benefits from richer, high-quality demonstrations. This raises the question of whether fairer baselines—e.g., a variant of DAgger that queries the expert on random perturbations—might close the performance gap.  
• Exploration of querying strategies: The manuscript does not investigate alternative query mechanisms beyond isotropic Gaussian noise. For instance, perturbing in directions informed by the expert’s local Jacobian or uncertainty estimates might yield further gains. A deeper study of query scheduling or adaptive perturbation magnitudes could strengthen the technical contribution.  
• Relation to prior data-augmentation RL work: Recent works like RAD [Laskin et al., 2020] systematically explore image-based augmentations in offline RL, and label-perturbation there is conceptually related. A more thorough comparison to these studies, especially in the vision + state setting, would situate APC more firmly within the literature.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Introduce a comparative baseline that also queries the expert on M perturbed states but trains with a different loss (e.g., square-error instead of log-likelihood) or with a structured perturbation pattern, to isolate the impact of sampling strategy versus loss form.  
2. Explore adaptive noise scales σs—perhaps tied to local state-space sensitivity or expert uncertainty—to see whether non-uniform perturbations can further reduce required queries.  
3. Expand discussion around connections to RAD-style image augmentations and clarify why state perturbations uniquely yield feedback-sensitivity rather than mere invariance (Section 1).  
4. Provide a small ablation in the Appendix where the Gaussian perturbation is replaced by, say, uniform or state-dependent bounds, to test robustness of the augmentation scheme.

Decision:
5: Borderline accept — technically solid paper with clear empirical benefits, although the idea is simple and could be strengthened with additional baseline comparisons and exploration of query strategies. The advantages of APC—particularly in data efficiency and expert-compression scenarios—outweigh the concerns about novelty and fairness of comparisons.

title: Unsupervised Representation Learning from Pre-trained Diffusion Probabilistic Models

reviews.summary: Whereas prior work (DiffAE) uses a conditional DPM as the decoder in an auto-encoder setup, this work attempts to leverage pretrained unconditional DPMs instead. The proposed formulation extends the classifier-guidance solution for latent autoencoder semantic codes z. However, instead of explicitly training classifiers p(z | x_t), taking its corresponding score, they propose to directly fit to the posterior mean gap of the pretrained, unsupervised, and frozen DPM. Results demonstrate higher quality reconstruction than DiffAE, with an overall simpler training setup.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
strengths
- paper was clearly written. Formulation was easy to follow
- prior works using classifier guidance had some issues regarding the scale of the guidance vector being used, but I believe the proposed formulation gets around that issue by fitting to the posterior mean gap instead.

weaknesses
- In section 3.4, it's not clear how the start and end of the critical stage are determined.
- L124 Except the latent code z (awkward phrasing)
- One thing that would've been nice to see is whether it's truly necessary to use another DPM for modeling the generative distribution $z$. DiffAE justifies their choice of a DPM by stating that a VAE's objective would have been difficult to tune, but perhaps a simple auto-encoder with a post-fit GMM over the latent vectors might suffice?
reviews.questions:
- See weaknesses
- How does the closeness between the pretrained DPM's domain and the target domain affect the efficacy of this method? If the pretrained DPM were from a completely unrelated domain, would this just mean that the posterior gap is larger and that the training time of this method would take much longer?
reviews.limitations: Limitations were not discussed. Example topics would include deep fakes etc.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:
Whereas prior work (DiffAE) uses a conditional DPM as the decoder in an auto-encoder setup, this work attempts to leverage pretrained unconditional DPMs instead. The proposed formulation extends the classifier-guidance solution for latent autoencoder semantic codes z. However, instead of explicitly training classifiers p(z | x_t), taking its corresponding score, they propose to directly fit to the posterior mean gap of the pretrained, unsupervised, and frozen DPM. Results demonstrate higher quality reconstruction than DiffAE, with an overall simpler training setup.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

- prior works using classifier guidance had some issues regarding the scale of the guidance vector being used, but I believe the proposed formulation gets around that issue by fitting to the posterior mean gap instead.

weaknesses

- L124 Except the latent code z (awkward phrasing)
- One thing that would've been nice to see is whether it's truly necessary to use another DPM for modeling the generative distribution $z$. DiffAE justifies their choice of a DPM by stating that a VAE's objective would have been difficult to tune, but perhaps a simple auto-encoder with a post-fit GMM over the latent vectors might suffice?

Questions:

- How does the closeness between the pretrained DPM's domain and the target domain affect the efficacy of this method? If the pretrained DPM were from a completely unrelated domain, would this just mean that the posterior gap is larger and that the training time of this method would take much longer?

reviews.summary:
The authors propose an unsupervised method for data representation and reconstruction, utilizing a pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DMP) in order to avoid the long training time of such models.
The method incorporates a gradient estimator, that learns the posterior mean shift, which is then used as a condition in order to guide the sampling process towards the image reconstruction. The authors also propose a new weighting scheme for training the objective function. Learning the gradient estimator encourages the encoder to learn meaningful data representation, and the sampling process to produce better data reconstruction.
The authors provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations showing improvements in representation learning, image reconstruction, and image sampling.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

- The problem is known to be valid and challenging, and the level of novelty is reasonable.

- The method, idea, and motivation are well explained in the paper. The authors provided a good intuition about their proposed network architecture.

- The authors well-explained their weighting method by describing their insights from an experiment that shows differences in the result with and without the classifier-guided sampling

Weaknesses:

- There is no algorithm that explains how the sampling step works in the author's method (how does the sampling-guided method is employed when using the model described in the paper).

- I suggest showing Figures 1,2 in separate locations (figure 2 should be closer to the text that describes it, along with figure 3).

- According to the quantitative results, the improvement of data representation is not significant. However, there is an improvement in the rest of the experiments, such as in unconditional sampling.

- Overall proofreading is required.
reviews.questions:
- Line 16: "and train it like a normal DMP": this causes some confusion, as the DMP part of the model is fixed. Also, the objective function aims to learn image reconstruction rather than image generation.

- It would be nice to see the results of the experiment shown in section 3.4, but when using the gradient-estimator as classifier-guided sampling, instead of a separate classifier, as done on MNIST.

- In the experiment section, the prediction of x_0 is done from x_t in only one step. What does this "one-step" mean? Isn't the whole reverse process required in order to retrieve x_0?

- It would be great to have an experiment that supports the claim that this method requires less training time than its competitors.
reviews.limitations:
The author did not address their method's limitations.
The described work has no potential negative societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Review for “Unsupervised Representation Learning from Pre-trained Diffusion Probabilistic Models”  

Summary:  
This paper introduces Pre-trained DPM AutoEncoding (PDAE), an unsupervised framework that adapts existing pre-trained diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) to serve as decoders in an autoencoding setup. Instead of training a diffusion model from scratch as in Diff-AE [36], the authors freeze most of a pre-trained unconditional DPM (µθ, Σθ) and learn a lightweight gradient estimator Gψ that predicts a “mean shift” Σθ·Gψ(xt, z, t) to fill the posterior mean gap caused by forward-process information loss. A simple encoder Eφ(x0) produces a compact semantic code z, and the predicted mean shift is used in classifier-guided fashion (Eq. 5) to reconstruct x0 or sample images conditioned on z. To stabilize training, they propose a novel weighting scheme λt (Eq. 10) based on signal-to-noise ratio that down-weights early and late diffusion timesteps, focusing representation learning on a critical middle stage. Experiments on FFHQ, CelebA-HQ, LSUN, and ImageNet64 show that PDAE (1) converges in less than half the training time of Diff-AE, (2) fills the posterior mean gap as evidenced by one-step denoising and ∥µtrue–µpred∥² curves (Fig. 5), (3) achieves competitive SSIM/LPIPS/MSE reconstruction (Table 1), (4) enables smooth z-interpolation and semantic attribute manipulation (Figs. 7–8), (5) provides truncation-like effects via scaling Gψ (Fig. 9), (6) yields improved few-shot conditional generation (Table 3), and (7) can boost unconditional sampling FID in late steps (Table 2). The code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Strengths:  
- The paper tackles a valid and challenging problem: how to leverage existing large-scale pre-trained DPMs for representation learning without incurring the high cost of training from scratch. This is both practically important and timely given the proliferation of public diffusion checkpoints.  
- The methodological contribution is clearly motivated. The insight that classifier-guided sampling performs a mean shift to reconstruct lost information is insightful, and the reverse idea—learning a model to predict that shift—provides a clean autoencoder interpretation. The connection between posterior mean gap and semantic representation is well articulated in Section 3.1.  
- The proposed weighting scheme in Section 3.4 is thoroughly justified. The three-stage experiment on MNIST (Fig. 3) convincingly demonstrates that only the critical middle timesteps require strong guidance, motivating the SNR-based λt (Fig. 4). This λt design is a thoughtful adaptation of P2-weighting [7] to the autoencoding objective.  
- The paper offers extensive experiments covering autoencoding, unconditional sampling, conditional sampling, few-shot generation, interpolation, and attribute manipulation. The breadth of evaluations (Tables 1–3, Figs. 5–9) supports the claims about efficiency, performance, and flexibility.  
- Reusing parts of the pre-trained U-Net (encoder and time embedding) to initialize Gψ is a clever engineering design that contributes to faster convergence and higher throughput (81.57 vs. 75.41 imgs/sec./A100).  

Weaknesses:  
- The manuscript omits a clear algorithmic description of the sampling process when using Gψ. While the text references Eq. 5 and Eq. 6, it would be helpful to include a pseudo-code block or flowchart that details how to interleave standard DDPM/DDIM steps with the gradient estimator, especially for readers less familiar with classifier-guided diffusion.  
- Figure placement could be improved. Figures 1 and 2 are referenced far from their definitions; relocating Figure 2 near the start of Section 3.3 and Figure 3 close to Section 3.4 would aid readability.  
- In the quantitative autoencoding metrics (Table 1), the improvement in SSIM and MSE over Diff-AE is modest (e.g., SSIM 0.696 vs. 0.677), and the LPIPS improvement is mixed. The margin of representation quality gain is relatively small compared to the other strong benefits (training time, sampling quality).  
- The paper would benefit from more rigorous proofreading. There are minor typos (e.g., “normal DMP” → “normal DPM” on Line 16), inconsistent notation in the objective (λt vs. Lsimple), and missing references to appendix figure numbers.  

Questions and Suggestions:  
1. Line 16 states “and train it like a normal DMP,” which may confuse readers since θ is frozen and only (ψ, φ) are trained. Perhaps rephrase to “we train (ψ, φ) with the standard DPM loss, treating θ as fixed.” Also clarify that the objective is autoencoding reconstruction, not unconditional generation.  
2. In Section 3.4, you demonstrate P2-style weighting using a separate classifier on MNIST. It would strengthen the paper to repeat that experiment by using the trained Gψ itself—i.e., perform mixed DDPM/DDPM-with-Gψ sampling only in the critical stage—and show that the mean-shift model alone suffices to correct class labels.  
3. The “one-step” denoising experiment in Section 4.2: please clarify what is meant by “denoising them for only one step.” Do you sample x0 directly from xt in a single reverse step using Eq. 4 or Eq. 6? A short paragraph in the main text would resolve this ambiguity.  
4. The claim of “less than half the training time” is based on throughput and iteration counts. Could you include a plot of training loss vs. wall-clock time for PDAE vs. Diff-AE to more concretely illustrate convergence speed?  
5. While your experiments demonstrate robust performance, a dedicated discussion of limitations would improve transparency. For instance, how sensitive is PDAE to the choice of pre-trained DPM architecture or dataset domain shifts between pre-training and fine-tuning?  
6. You note no obvious negative societal impacts, but since diffusion models can generate highly realistic images, a brief statement on ethical usage or detection of deepfakes could be appropriate.

Minor Points:  
- In Algorithm 1 (Appendix), please ensure all variables (e.g., σt, ϵt) are defined.  
- On page 7, Table 2 has misaligned rows for “Bedroom” and “CelebA”—please fix formatting.  
- In related work, explicitly contrast your method with the concurrent textual inversion [12], drawing the posterior gap analogy.

Overall, this paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach for reusing pre-trained diffusion models in representation learning. Despite some presentation and minor experimental clarity issues, the contributions are substantive: linking poster mean gap to representation, a novel SNR-based weighting scheme, and demonstrating significant training efficiency gains. I recommend a Weak Accept.

Rating: 6: Weak Accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose an unsupervised method for data representation and reconstruction, utilizing a pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DMP) in order to avoid the long training time of such models.
The method incorporates a gradient estimator, that learns the posterior mean shift, which is then used as a condition in order to guide the sampling process towards the image reconstruction. The authors also propose a new weighting scheme for training the objective function. Learning the gradient estimator encourages the encoder to learn meaningful data representation, and the sampling process to produce better data reconstruction.
The authors provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations showing improvements in representation learning, image reconstruction, and image sampling.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

- The problem is known to be valid and challenging, and the level of novelty is reasonable.

- The method, idea, and motivation are well explained in the paper. The authors provided a good intuition about their proposed network architecture.

- The authors well-explained their weighting method by describing their insights from an experiment that shows differences in the result with and without the classifier-guided sampling

Weaknesses:

- There is no algorithm that explains how the sampling step works in the author's method (how does the sampling-guided method is employed when using the model described in the paper).

- I suggest showing Figures 1,2 in separate locations (figure 2 should be closer to the text that describes it, along with figure 3).

- According to the quantitative results, the improvement of data representation is not significant. However, there is an improvement in the rest of the experiments, such as in unconditional sampling.

- Overall proofreading is required.

Questions:
- Line 16: "and train it like a normal DMP": this causes some confusion, as the DMP part of the model is fixed. Also, the objective function aims to learn image reconstruction rather than image generation.

- It would be nice to see the results of the experiment shown in section 3.4, but when using the gradient-estimator as classifier-guided sampling, instead of a separate classifier, as done on MNIST.

- In the experiment section, the prediction of x_0 is done from x_t in only one step. What does this "one-step" mean? Isn't the whole reverse process required in order to retrieve x_0?

- It would be great to have an experiment that supports the claim that this method requires less training time than its competitors.

Limitations:
The author did not address their method's limitations.
The described work has no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a new learning framework for diffusion probabilistic models. Unlike the most existing diffusion probabilistic models, which mainly embed the data into a predefined distribution through the Markov chain, the proposed framework aims to learn a robust representation feature through a pre-trained diffusion models. The authors also introduce a weighting scheme redesign function to further improve the representation learning in the diffusion models. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple scenarios (e.g. FFHQ, CelebA, Bedroom).
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths
- The task of representation learning via diffusion probabilistic models is novel and interesting.
- The overall idea and the approach towards addressing it seems reasonable. 

### Weaknesses
- The paper is not well written and hard to read.
- The technical novelty is limited. Neither the designed network nor the theory are new. 
- The qualitative results are poor, and the quantitative improvement is limited.
reviews.questions:
- What's the key difference between the proposed framework with the latest state-of-the-art diffusion autoencoders (Diff-AE) [21]. While the authors consider Diff-AE as a baseline, the two works seem to be similar. The authors should clearly explain what is the difference between them.
- Besides, The qualitative comparison to the Diff-AE baseline is missing, even after considering the results in the Appendix. What's more, the quantitative improvement in Tables 1, 2, and 3 is limited compared to the  Diff-AE baseline.
- It takes me a hard time buying the $x_0\to x_t$ in Figure 1. If I understand correctly, this should be a diffusion processing in general. Then, does this contain any pre-trained parameters?
- Could please the authors illuminate the difference between these results shown in Figure 5? While the authors claimed to "some stochastic variations", it seems to be very hard to capture such a conclusion.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are not discussed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a new learning framework for diffusion probabilistic models. Unlike the most existing diffusion probabilistic models, which mainly embed the data into a predefined distribution through the Markov chain, the proposed framework aims to learn a robust representation feature through a pre-trained diffusion models. The authors also introduce a weighting scheme redesign function to further improve the representation learning in the diffusion models. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple scenarios (e.g. FFHQ, CelebA, Bedroom).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths
- The task of representation learning via diffusion probabilistic models is novel and interesting.
- The overall idea and the approach towards addressing it seems reasonable. 

### Weaknesses
- The paper is not well written and hard to read.
- The technical novelty is limited. Neither the designed network nor the theory are new. 
- The qualitative results are poor, and the quantitative improvement is limited.

Questions:
- What's the key difference between the proposed framework with the latest state-of-the-art diffusion autoencoders (Diff-AE) [21]. While the authors consider Diff-AE as a baseline, the two works seem to be similar. The authors should clearly explain what is the difference between them.
- Besides, The qualitative comparison to the Diff-AE baseline is missing, even after considering the results in the Appendix. What's more, the quantitative improvement in Tables 1, 2, and 3 is limited compared to the  Diff-AE baseline.
- It takes me a hard time buying the $x_0\to x_t$ in Figure 1. If I understand correctly, this should be a diffusion processing in general. Then, does this contain any pre-trained parameters?
- Could please the authors illuminate the difference between these results shown in Figure 5? While the authors claimed to "some stochastic variations", it seems to be very hard to capture such a conclusion.

Limitations:
Limitations are not discussed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:
The manuscript “Unsupervised Representation Learning from Pre-trained Diffusion Probabilistic Models” proposes a novel framework, termed Pre-trained DPM AutoEncoding (PDAE), which adapts existing pre-trained diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) into an autoencoder-style decoder for image reconstruction. Instead of training a diffusion model from scratch as in Diffusion Autoencoders (Diff-AE), the authors leverage a frozen, pre-trained unconditional DPM pθ(xt−1|xt) and introduce a gradient estimator Gψ(xt, z, t) that predicts a “mean shift” term to fill the posterior mean gap caused by the forward diffusion process (Sec. 3.1). The encoder Eφ(x0) produces a semantic latent code z, which is then used by Gψ to guide the reverse DPM sampling (Eq. 7). The paper also redesigns the weighting scheme λt of the diffusion loss based on signal-to-noise ratio (Eq. 10) to focus representation learning on the critical stage of the diffusion trajectory (Fig. 3 and Fig. 4). Extensive experiments on FFHQ, CelebA(-HQ), LSUN Bedroom, LSUN Horse, and ImageNet demonstrate that PDAE achieves similar or slightly improved reconstruction and sampling metrics compared to Diff-AE while using less training time and fewer training batches (Sec. 4.1–4.8). The paper draws a conceptual connection between filling the posterior mean gap and classifier-guided sampling methods, and even explains concurrent textual inversion approaches under this framework (Sec. 5).

Strengths:
- Novelty of Task: The paper tackles the relatively under-explored problem of unsupervised representation learning via diffusion probabilistic models, extending beyond purely generative uses of DPMs to learn semantic codes. This is an interesting direction given the recent surge of work on diffusion-based generative modeling.
- Reasonable Approach: The core idea of predicting a mean shift to compensate for information loss in the forward diffusion process is well motivated (Sec. 3.2). By reusing pre-trained diffusion networks and freezing θ, the method cleverly reduces compute while guiding the encoder to capture meaningful features that directly correlate with missing conditional information.
- Training Efficiency: The reported training throughput of 81.57 imgs/sec./A100 for PDAE versus 75.41 for Diff-AE, and the fact that PDAE converges in roughly one half to one third the number of batches (Sec. 4.1), underlines a practical benefit of leveraging pre-trained models. The reuse of the U-Net encoder and redesigned λt (Fig. 4) concretely contributes to this efficiency.
- Conceptual Connections: Interpreting classifier-guided sampling as filling the posterior mean gap (Eq. 5) provides new insights that tie together conditional diffusion, guidance methods, and representation learning. The paper also bridges to textual inversion in text-to-image DPMs (Sec. 5), suggesting a unifying perspective.

Weaknesses:
- Clarity and Readability: The manuscript is not as well written as it could be, and certain sections are hard to follow. For example, the derivation of Eq. 7 from the variational bound requires more detailed explanation, and the transitions between theoretical motivation and algorithmic implementation in Sec. 3.2 can be abrupt. The notation around the mean shift (µθ + Σθ·Gψ) is introduced quickly without full unpacking.
- Limited Technical Novelty: While the idea of mean shift estimation is sound, the underlying network components (standard convolutional encoder, U-Net with AdaGN blocks in Eq. 9) and the weighting scheme adaptation (inspired by P2-weighting [7], Eq. 10) are incremental rather than fundamentally new. There is no novel theoretical guarantee beyond the practical insight about posterior mean gaps.
- Modest Quantitative Gains: Across the reconstruction experiments (Table 1 on CelebA-HQ), sampling FIDs (Table 2), and few-shot conditional generation (Table 3), the improvements over Diff-AE are relatively small. For instance, PDAE @64M on CelebA-HQ achieves SSIM of 0.696 versus 0.677 for Diff-AE @130M, but LPIPS and MSE differences are minor. Similarly, FID improvements for unconditional sampling on FFHQ (10.31 vs. 10.55 at T=100) are limited.
- Qualitative Results Quality: Some of the visual examples, particularly for autoencoding reconstructions in Fig. 6, appear blurred or lack sharpness in minor details compared to the baseline. The stochastic variations claimed (e.g., hair wisps changing under random xT in Fig. 6) are subtle and not convincingly illustrated across multiple samples.

Questions and Requests for Clarification:
1. Relation to Diff-AE: Beyond leveraging a pre-trained DPM, what is the key architectural or algorithmic difference between PDAE and the state-of-the-art Diffusion Autoencoder [36]? The authors label Diff-AE as the primary baseline, but the core paradigm—encoder plus conditional DPM decoder—appears very similar. A side-by-side schematic or bullet comparison of differences (e.g., loss functions, reuse of weights, training schedules) would be helpful.
2. Qualitative Comparison: Although quantitative tables include Diff-AE results, a direct qualitative side-by-side comparison of reconstructed images from PDAE and Diff-AE (perhaps in an appendix figure) is missing. Providing such visual comparisons under identical settings (same xT, same sampling method) would clarify the perceptual gains.
3. Figure 1 Clarification: It is stated that Figure 1 compares diffusion loss between unconditional and conditional DPMs trained on MNIST. However, it is unclear whether the conditional model uses a pre-trained MNIST classifier or is trained jointly. Does this experiment involve any pre-trained parameters, or is it purely learned from scratch? Clarify what “conditional DPM” entails in this context.
4. Interpretation of Figure 5: The authors claim “some stochastic variations” in the one-step denoising reconstructions (first row pre-trained DPM, second row PDAE). In practice, how significant are these variations, and how consistent are they across different random seeds? A statistical measure (e.g., variance in LPIPS over multiple runs) would bolster this qualitative statement.
5. Limitations Section: The manuscript currently lacks an explicit discussion of limitations. For instance, how sensitive is PDAE to the choice of pre-trained model quality, to the hyperparameter γ in Eq. 10, or to the dimensionality of z? Please add a paragraph acknowledging such potential limitations.

Maintained Decision:
5: Borderline accept. Despite the readability issues, limited novelty in network design, and only modest gains over Diff-AE, the paper provides a reasonable new perspective on leveraging pre-trained diffusion models for representation learning. The practical efficiency improvements and conceptual connections to classifier-guidance and textual inversion make it a technically solid contribution where reasons to accept (novel perspective, efficiency gains, comprehensive experiments) slightly outweigh reasons to reject.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a method of learning representations out-of pretrained unconditional diffusion models. Different from DiffusionAE which learns the encoder and condition DPM together, this paper proposes to keep a pretrained unconditional diffusion models unchanged, and learn the gradient of classifier guidance which takes a latent variable as the additional input, and the latent variable in encoded by a learnable encoder. Empirical results show that this method leads to better reconstruction and few-shot conditional generation results compared to DiffusionAE with less training expense. Qualitatively the method learns meaningful representations, and it can also improve the sample quality of unconditional diffusion models with a learned latent DPM on the latent variables.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

- This paper is well written and easy to follow. It clearly states its connection with the previous close related work, DiffusionAE, and points out the advantage over the previous work. 
- The proposed method is technically sound and naturally combines learning representation and classifier guidance. 
- Various experiments are carried out and results are compared with baseline methods, making the method more convincing. 

Weaknesses: 
- IMO the novelty of this work is kind of limited. Compared to DiffusionAE, this work includes the classifier guidance and the latent codes are embedded into the learnable guidance term instead of the diffusion model, expect for which all other components remains the same as DiffusionAE, such as reversing DDIM sampler for latent code and learning latent DPM for z. Technically speaking I don't think there's a significant novel method proposed here.
- Compared to DiffusionAE, this method includes guidance term so it is not quite surprising to me that it can outperform DiffusionAE. It's not clear to me whether the improvement is due to the guidance mechanism or due to the novel formulation $G_\psi$.
reviews.questions:
- One baseline worthwhile to compare is DiffusionAE + classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method can beat this baseline, it will be more convincing that the improvement is given by the novel formulation $G_\psi$, instead of the guidance formulation which is not the contribution of this paper. 

- For classifier(-free) guidance, it is known that results are better if the diffusion models are also conditional, i.e., the modified score is given by $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ instead of $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$. I'm wondering if similar conclusion applies here. I.e., if you condition both the diffusion model and the classifier gradient $G_\psi$ with the latent variables $z$, whether you can get better performance than the current formulation.
reviews.limitations: No limitation and potential negative social impact are discussed .
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a method of learning representations out-of pretrained unconditional diffusion models. Different from DiffusionAE which learns the encoder and condition DPM together, this paper proposes to keep a pretrained unconditional diffusion models unchanged, and learn the gradient of classifier guidance which takes a latent variable as the additional input, and the latent variable in encoded by a learnable encoder. Empirical results show that this method leads to better reconstruction and few-shot conditional generation results compared to DiffusionAE with less training expense. Qualitatively the method learns meaningful representations, and it can also improve the sample quality of unconditional diffusion models with a learned latent DPM on the latent variables.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

- This paper is well written and easy to follow. It clearly states its connection with the previous close related work, DiffusionAE, and points out the advantage over the previous work. 
- The proposed method is technically sound and naturally combines learning representation and classifier guidance. 
- Various experiments are carried out and results are compared with baseline methods, making the method more convincing. 

Weaknesses: 
- IMO the novelty of this work is kind of limited. Compared to DiffusionAE, this work includes the classifier guidance and the latent codes are embedded into the learnable guidance term instead of the diffusion model, expect for which all other components remains the same as DiffusionAE, such as reversing DDIM sampler for latent code and learning latent DPM for z. Technically speaking I don't think there's a significant novel method proposed here.
- Compared to DiffusionAE, this method includes guidance term so it is not quite surprising to me that it can outperform DiffusionAE. It's not clear to me whether the improvement is due to the guidance mechanism or due to the novel formulation $G_\psi$.

Questions:
- One baseline worthwhile to compare is DiffusionAE + classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method can beat this baseline, it will be more convincing that the improvement is given by the novel formulation $G_\psi$, instead of the guidance formulation which is not the contribution of this paper. 

- For classifier(-free) guidance, it is known that results are better if the diffusion models are also conditional, i.e., the modified score is given by $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ instead of $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$. I'm wondering if similar conclusion applies here. I.e., if you condition both the diffusion model and the classifier gradient $G_\psi$ with the latent variables $z$, whether you can get better performance than the current formulation.

Limitations:
No limitation and potential negative social impact are discussed .

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review for “Unsupervised Representation Learning from Pre-trained Diffusion Probabilistic Models”  

Summary:  
This paper proposes a technique, PDAE, for unsupervised representation learning by adapting a frozen, pre-trained unconditional diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) into an autoencoder framework. Instead of training both encoder and conditional DPM from scratch (as in Diffusion-AE [36]), the authors freeze the pre-trained unconditional DPM weights and learn only a lightweight “gradient estimator” network Gψ that predicts an additive mean shift to fill the posterior mean gap in the reverse diffusion process. The encoder Eφ maps each input x₀ to a latent code z, and Gψ( x_t, z, t ) serves as a classifier-like guidance term to push the DPM’s predicted reverse mean closer to the true posterior mean. A new weighting schedule based on signal-to-noise ratio (Eq.10) further stabilizes training. Empirical results on FFHQ, CelebA-HQ, LSUN Horse and Bedroom demonstrate that PDAE (1) achieves higher reconstruction quality (SSIM/LPIPS/MSE) than Diffusion-AE with less than half the training time, (2) learns meaningful latent spaces that support smooth interpolation and attribute manipulation, (3) improves few-shot conditional generation, and (4) can boost unconditional sample quality of pre-trained DPMs via a learned latent DPM.  

Strengths:  
- Well written and clear presentation. The paper carefully motivates the use of pre-trained DPMs (Sec.1), explains the origin of the “posterior mean gap” via information loss in the forward process (Sec.3.1), and draws a neat connection to classifier-guided sampling (Eqs.5–6).  
- Technically sound idea. By freezing θ and learning only Gψ and Eφ, the method reuses expensive pre-training, reduces optimization burden and allows efficient fine-tuning. The U-Net reuse strategy (Sec.3.3) and the AdaGN conditioning (Eq.9) cleverly inject z without retraining all layers.  
- Careful design of the weighting scheme (Sec.3.4, Fig.4) shows that down-weighting early and late diffusion steps stabilizes training, mirroring P2-weighting insights [7].  
- Extensive experiments and comparisons with Diffusion-AE and other generative models. The reconstruction metrics on CelebA-HQ (Table 1), FID for unconditional sampling (Table 2), interpolation examples (Fig. 7), attribute editing (Fig. 8), truncation-like effect (Fig. 9) and few-shot conditional results (Table 3) form a convincing empirical case. The reported ≈1.08× throughput improvement (81.57 vs. 75.41 imgs/sec/A100) and ≈2× fewer batches to convergence are valuable.  

Weaknesses:  
- Limited novelty over Diffusion-AE. Beyond moving the conditioning from the reverse DPM to a separate gradient estimator Gψ, most other components (DDIM sampling, latent DPM on z, autoencoding framework) remain unchanged. In technical terms, the paper substitutes explicit conditional U-Net tuning with a classifier-like guidance module, but the overall pipeline closely mirrors that of Diffusion-AE.  
- It is not entirely clear whether the performance gains stem from the new mean-shift parameterization Gψ(·) or simply from the use of classifier guidance. The real contribution appears to be applying guidance in the unsupervised setting, but the authors should isolate the effect of Gψ’s architecture versus the generic benefit of adding guidance to Diffusion-AE.  
- No ablation comparing to Diffusion-AE equipped with standard classifier-free guidance. Since classifier-free diffusion guidance [15] is now a canonical way to improve sample and reconstruction quality, it would strengthen the claim to show that PDAE still outperforms Diffusion-AE + classifier-free guidance under the same training budget.  
- Relatedly, modern guidance schemes often condition both the score model and the classifier term on c (i.e. ∇ₓ [ log pθ(x_t|c) + ω log pϕ(c|x_t) ]). It remains an open question whether conditioning both the diffusion model θ and the gradient estimator Gψ on the latent z yields further gains, or whether PDAE’s decoupled approach already matches that.  

Questions & Suggestions:  
1. A worthwhile baseline would be “Diffusion-AE + classifier-free guidance” at ω=1.0 and ω>1.0, using the same U-Net and training iterations. Can PDAE still outperform that stronger baseline? This would clarify if the novel formulation Gψ is indeed the major factor.  
2. Have the authors experimented with conditioning the original diffusion model θ on z (i.e. training a small adapter in the pre-trained DPM with frozen U-Net backbone) in addition to Gψ? If so, how does that compare?  
3. The choice of γ=0.1 in the P2-style weighting (Eq.10) was set empirically. Could the paper include an ablation study varying γ to show robustness? A plot of reconstruction loss versus γ would be informative.  
4. In Sec.4.4 on semantic interpolation, the observed linearity of Gψ in z (Fig.7) is compelling. A quantitative measure—e.g. the average cosine similarity between Gψ(xt, z₁ₗₑᵣₚ, t) and Lerp(Gψ(xt, z₁, t), Gψ(xt, z₂, t); λ)—would further support the claim of linear latent structure.  
5. The discussion on societal impact is minimal. Since DPMs can generate hyper‐realistic faces and artworks, a brief note on potential misuse (deepfakes, copyright issues) and energy cost of diffusion sampling would complete the ethical perspective.  

Overall, this paper presents a well-engineered and empirically validated approach for representation learning that leverages pre-trained diffusion models. While the core idea is closely related to Diffusion-AE with added guidance, the work demonstrates substantial practical benefits: faster convergence, improved reconstruction and sampling, and multiple downstream applications. The paper is recommended for acceptance as a borderline accept (rating: 5), given its solid technical execution and extensive experiments, despite limited methodological novelty.

Summary:
This paper presents a method of learning representations out-of pretrained unconditional diffusion models. Different from DiffusionAE which learns the encoder and condition DPM together, this paper proposes to keep a pretrained unconditional diffusion models unchanged, and learn the gradient of classifier guidance which takes a latent variable as the additional input, and the latent variable in encoded by a learnable encoder. Empirical results show that this method leads to better reconstruction and few-shot conditional generation results compared to DiffusionAE with less training expense. Qualitatively the method learns meaningful representations, and it can also improve the sample quality of unconditional diffusion models with a learned latent DPM on the latent variables.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strength:

- This paper is well written and easy to follow. It clearly states its connection with the previous close related work, DiffusionAE, and points out the advantage over the previous work. 
- The proposed method is technically sound and naturally combines learning representation and classifier guidance. 
- Various experiments are carried out and results are compared with baseline methods, making the method more convincing. 

Weaknesses: 
- IMO the novelty of this work is kind of limited. Compared to DiffusionAE, this work includes the classifier guidance and the latent codes are embedded into the learnable guidance term instead of the diffusion model, expect for which all other components remains the same as DiffusionAE, such as reversing DDIM sampler for latent code and learning latent DPM for z. Technically speaking I don't think there's a significant novel method proposed here.
- Compared to DiffusionAE, this method includes guidance term so it is not quite surprising to me that it can outperform DiffusionAE. It's not clear to me whether the improvement is due to the guidance mechanism or due to the novel formulation $G_\psi$.

Questions:
- One baseline worthwhile to compare is DiffusionAE + classifier-free guidance. If the proposed method can beat this baseline, it will be more convincing that the improvement is given by the novel formulation $G_\psi$, instead of the guidance formulation which is not the contribution of this paper. 

- For classifier(-free) guidance, it is known that results are better if the diffusion models are also conditional, i.e., the modified score is given by $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t|c) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$ instead of $\nabla_{x_t} [\log p(x_t) + \omega \log p(c|x_t)]$. I'm wondering if similar conclusion applies here. I.e., if you condition both the diffusion model and the classifier gradient $G_\psi$ with the latent variables $z$, whether you can get better performance than the current formulation.

Limitations:
No limitation and potential negative social impact are discussed .

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: S$^3$-NeRF: Neural Reflectance Field from Shading and Shadow under a Single Viewpoint

reviews.summary:
This pape proposed a method that aims at solving the near-field photometric stereo via neural reflectance field representation. The core insight of this paper is to use both the shading and cast-shadows of the view as the cues to recover both the visible and invisible parts of a scene. 
The paper adopts the NeRF-like neural field representation to represent the occupancy field, reflectance field of a scene. They then use lighting, BRDF modeling and shadow rendering to reconstruct the observed images. The networks are jointly optimized via volume rendering loss, smoothness loss and color loss.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed method can estimate the geometry of the invisible region of the scene from a single-view point. The paper is interesting in estimating invisible regions from the shadows. And unlike previous shadow-based methods, this paper doesn't require explicitly shadow detection as input.
- The method presents state-of-the-art geometry reconstruction results compare to prior near-field photometric stereo methods. 
Each component of the method, such as shadow and shading, is validated in the ablation studies.
- The paper is well writen and easy to follow. 

Weaknesses
- Each part of the proposed method is not novel. The method consists of three major parts: neural reflectance field representation follows [38] in occupancy field estimation and normal computation; the color and BRDF modeling follow [15,24] to use the sphere gaussian bases, SG weights and diffuse albedo; the shadow computation is slightly different to [48], but it is similar to [24] where they both check the visibility of the surface point. Hence, in the proposed method and rendering procedure of this paper, the novelty is very limited.

- It is suggested to evaluate this method in more challenging real and synthetic datasets. The method was only tested on two datasets: a simple synthetic dataset and a real dataset. For the synthetic dataset: the background is clean and uniform; how will this paper perform in backgrounds with more textures?

- I am curious to see how the number of lights affects the accuracy of the geometry in the invisible part of the scene. The only cues for invisible regions are the casted shadows on the ground. If lights fail to illuminate the invisible regions from different angles, how will the accuracy be affected?
reviews.questions: Please see the quesitons in the Weaknesses part.
reviews.limitations:
- Limitations are well discussed in the paper.
- Suggestions: It will be great to isolate the invisible region of the scene and show quantitatively how the proposed shadow rendering improve the accuracy in these regions.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

They then use lighting, BRDF modeling and shadow rendering to reconstruct the observed images. The networks are jointly optimized via volume rendering loss, smoothness loss and color loss.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed method can estimate the geometry of the invisible region of the scene from a single-view point. The paper is interesting in estimating invisible regions from the shadows. And unlike previous shadow-based methods, this paper doesn't require explicitly shadow detection as input.
- The method presents state-of-the-art geometry reconstruction results compare to prior near-field photometric stereo methods. 
Each component of the method, such as shadow and shading, is validated in the ablation studies.
- The paper is well writen and easy to follow. 

Weaknesses
- Each part of the proposed method is not novel. The method consists of three major parts: neural reflectance field representation follows [38] in occupancy field estimation and normal computation; the color and BRDF modeling follow [15,24] to use the sphere gaussian bases, SG weights and diffuse albedo; the shadow computation is slightly different to [48], but it is similar to [24] where they both check the visibility of the surface point. Hence, in the proposed method and rendering procedure of this paper, the novelty is very limited.

- It is suggested to evaluate this method in more challenging real and synthetic datasets. The method was only tested on two datasets: a simple synthetic dataset and a real dataset. For the synthetic dataset: the background is clean and uniform; how will this paper perform in backgrounds with more textures?

- I am curious to see how the number of lights affects the accuracy of the geometry in the invisible part of the scene. The only cues for invisible regions are the casted shadows on the ground. If lights fail to illuminate the invisible regions from different angles, how will the accuracy be affected?

Questions:
Please see the quesitons in the Weaknesses part.

Limitations:
- Limitations are well discussed in the paper.
- Suggestions: It will be great to isolate the invisible region of the scene and show quantitatively how the proposed shadow rendering improve the accuracy in these regions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “S3-NeRF: Neural Reflectance Field from Shading and Shadow under a Single Viewpoint”

Summary  
This paper addresses near-field photometric stereo by learning a neural reflectance field from single-view images captured under varying point lights. Building on NeRF-like occupancy and radiance fields, the method explicitly models BRDFs with diffuse albedo and sphere‐gaussian (SG) bases, and computes shadows online via differentiable visibility checks at surface points. The network is optimized end-to-end using volume rendering losses, surface‐rendering color losses, and normal‐smoothness regularization. Extensive experiments on multiple synthetic objects (e.g., BUDDHA, BUNNY, CHAIR) and real captures demonstrate the method’s ability to recover both visible and occluded geometry, as well as support novel‐view synthesis and relighting.

Strengths  
1. Recovering Invisible Geometry via Shadow Cues  
   - The key technical insight—that cast shadows provide strong constraints on occluded or back‐facing regions—enables reconstruction of complete 3D geometry from a single viewpoint. For example, in Fig. 8 the READING object’s concave backside is well recovered, even though only its shadow boundary on the desk is observed.  
   - Unlike prior shape‐from‐shadow methods [11,61,67] that require pre‐detected shadow masks, this work integrates shadow computation in the rendering pipeline without explicit shadow segmentation, improving robustness to noisy shadow boundaries.

2. State‐of‐the‐Art Accuracy against Near‐Field PS and Neural Field Baselines  
   - Quantitatively, the proposed S3-NeRF achieves an average normal MAE of 2.84° on synthetic scenes, significantly outperforming NeRF∗ (70° MAE) and UNISURF∗ (54.9° MAE) baselines (Table 1). It also improves depth L1 error from 54.27 cm to 1.72 cm on BUNNY when compared to UNISURF∗.  
   - Compared against classical single-view photometric stereo methods QY18 [40] and HS20 [44], which assume directional or near-field lights, S3-NeRF reduces normal MAE by up to 50% and depth L1 by similar margins (Table 2). These results fully validate that explicit volumetric representation and shadow modeling yield more accurate geometry than 2.5D normal/depth maps.

3. Thorough Component Ablation  
   - The ablation study on shading (“w/o shading”) and shadow (“w/o shadow”) modules (Table 3) clearly demonstrates that neither cue alone suffices to reconstruct the full shape: without shadow, occluded regions collapse, and without shading, the model fails altogether. Adding the surface‐rendering loss Ls further refines normals by about 10% (Fig. 6).  
   - The analysis of light number and distribution (Table 4, 5; Fig. 9) shows that at least 8 randomly placed near-field lights are required for stable reconstruction, and that performance improves only marginally beyond 64 lights, indicating practical efficiency.

4. Clear Presentation and Reproducibility  
   - The paper is well‐written and structured. The main algorithm (Sec. 3) is explained step‐by‐step, from occupancy MLP to physics‐based color rendering (Eqn. 3–5) and online shadow computation (Eqn. 6–7).  
   - Implementation details, training schedules (800 epochs on RTX3090), network widths, and optimizer settings are thoroughly documented. Code and trained models are publicly released, enabling reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty of Components  
   - While the integration of volumetric neural fields, SG‐based BRDFs, and shadow visibility checks is well‐engineered, each building block closely follows existing work: occupancy‐based surface modeling [39], SG reflectance parameterization [16,25], and surface visibility sampling akin to prior differentiable rendering [49,71]. The paper’s main novelty lies in their combination for single-view photometric stereo, rather than in fundamentally new algorithms.  

2. Dataset Diversity and Background Complexity  
   - The synthetic scenes used for evaluation invariably feature uniform or planar backgrounds (desk and wall), which simplify shadow geometry and occlusion patterns. It remains unclear how the method performs with cluttered or textured backgrounds where indirect shadows and interreflections may confuse the occupancy and visibility predictions. Adding scenes with photographic textures or multiple occluders would strengthen the evaluation.

3. Sensitivity to Light Calibration and Placement  
   - The method assumes known point‐light positions and intensities. While real‐scene results use SDPS‐Net for self-calibration, accuracy of recovered lights is not quantified, and it is unclear how light estimation errors propagate to geometry reconstruction. Moreover, as only cast shadows drive reconstruction of unseen regions, insufficient angular coverage of lights (e.g., lights clustered in one hemisphere) might leave parts of the object unconstrained, a scenario not fully explored.

4. Quantitative Assessment of Invisible Regions  
   - Although the paper qualitatively shows recovery of occluded surfaces, a quantitative metric isolating the invisible regions (e.g., MAE restricted to back faces) is not reported. Including such a metric would more directly support the claim that shadows enable reconstruction of non-visible geometry.

Suggestions for Improvement  
- Introduce synthetic test cases with textured or cluttered backgrounds and measure performance drop, if any.  
- Provide quantitative light‐calibration accuracy and analyze its impact on final geometry errors.  
- Report MAE or IoU specifically on invisible/back‐facing surfaces to isolate the contribution of shadow cues.  
- Discuss potential extensions to handle indirect illumination or interreflections, which currently are ignored but may affect shadows in complex scenes.

Decision: Weak Accept (6)  
This paper presents a technically sound method that extends neural radiance/reflectance fields to the challenging single-view, near-field photometric stereo setting. Despite limited novelty in individual components, the unified framework compellingly demonstrates that shading and shadow cues can recover complete 3D geometry from a single viewpoint—a result of moderate to high impact for both photometric stereo and neural rendering communities. The evaluation is strong on synthetic and real data, though further tests on more complex backgrounds and light arrangements would bolster robustness claims. Overall, I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a single-view scene reconstruction method under different point light conditions based on the neural reflectance field. It uses the inverse-square law and BRDF to model the shading and root finding to model the shadow. By explicitly utilizing both shading and shadow cues, the model can reconstruct the scene from single view, even for some invisible regions.
The authors evaluated the model with different objects and showed that the method can well reconstruct the geometry with images from single view but different point lights.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

A novel NeRF-based method that faithfully reconstructs a scene from a fixed viewpoint with multiple single point light conditions by explicitly modeling the shading and shadow. By carefully modeling the single point light shading by inverse-square law and BRDF, and shadow by root-finding. This method gives an excellent result.
The paper clearly illustrated the method in detail. The authors compared their method with other methods quantitatively and qualitatively. They also evaluated their model with multiple occluding objects, showing it can reconstruct the unseen area with shading and shadow cues.
 
### Weakness

The authors studied the contribution of different components (shading and shadow) of their method. But the impact of different loss terms was not included. Specially, at L171, how the value of $\alpha$ for normal smoothness loss is found is not illustrated.
reviews.questions: How’s the normal smoothness loss affect your method? Is that similar to UNISURF? From your result, especially on LEGO, the loss of detail of studs and track seems very serious. Is that caused by normal smoothness loss or by more complicated light reflection at those areas?
reviews.limitations: Yes, the authors addressed different aspect of limitations of the work in the paper and supplementary materials. They also pointed out this method can recover some non-directly seen area, that may address some privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
